title: EDS 411: MEDS Capstone Courses
text:
Welcome to the course materials for MEDS Capstone Courses EDS 411A and EDS 411B! This course sequence is part of the UCSB Masters in Environmental Data Science at the Bren School of Environmental Science and Mananagement.
In this website you will find the materials covered during lectures for the 2024 Winter and Spring terms.


The goal of MEDS Capstone Courses EDS 411A and EDS 411B is to facilitate student learning, work, and progress on their MEDS Capstone project through lessons, activities, supervised and unsupervised project work, and peer review and feedback. Additionally, these courses provides opportunities for professional and career development (e.g. presentation skills, writing and editing, client relations, team science, panel discussions, and conflict resolution).

title: Week 7
text:
📕 Class slides
🔄 Feedback grid for Block 1
🔄 Feedback grid for Block 2
📗 Responding to questions slides - Dr. Allison Horst



The class will be divided in two blocks from 1:10 to 2:30 and from 2:30 to 4:00. Each team will present and provide feedback to other teams as follows:

Your theam should work on their project outside of their “presentation block”. The following rooms are reserved for you so you have a dedicated workspace during our usual class time. You’re welcome to use other places to work.











Reminder



Have you let clients, external advisors, friends or family you want to invite know when your final presentation is?







When? Due on Friday, May 16 at 5 PM.
How? Upload to this Box folder as Word (.doc) or Google doc format, not PDF.


Instructions

Abstracts and acknowledgments will be posted to the Bren website on your individual project pages and linked to the digital event program.

Upload your abstract and acknowledgments in a single file to this Box folder.
Include your group name in the file name.
Word (.doc) or Google doc format preferred, not PDF (must be copy & paste-able).

Abstract instructions:

The abstract is a one-paragraph brief summary of the Capstone Project, meant for a non-technical audience. The length of the abstract should be roughly 200 words. It can be the same as the one in your Technical Documentation.
If your project title has changed, please include the new title in the abstract.
Your advisor must approve the final abstract and any title updates before submission.

Acknowledgments instructions:

The acknowledgements should include advisors, professionals, organizations, funding sources, etc. that assisted the Capstone Project.
Each acknowledgment should include the person’s name, title (if known), and affiliation or organization.
You should obtain authorization from each person or party being acknowledged that their names can be included in the abstract.
Be consistent when listing your acknowledgments. You can list several people within an organization. Alphabetical order is preferred if listing several people in the same grouping.

Example 1: Joan Dudney, Assistant Professor, Bren School
Example 2: Bren School: Max Czapanskiy, Assistant Teaching Professor; Roland Geyer, Professor; Ruth Oliver, Assistant Professor
Example 3: The Nature Conservancy: Kelly Easterday, Heather Gately, Moses Katkowski, Diego Ortiz, Dr. Mark Reynolds, and Dr. Rae Wynn-Grant


Tip: You can view Bren faculty titles here. Also, if you have an email from someone, you may find their title in their email signature.





When? Due by next class Wednesday, May 21.
How? Add slides to shared drive.


Instructions


Assess or incorporate the feedback you received today and streamline your presentation. Iterate with your faculty advisor, TA, instructor, as needed.
You got this! Practice is everything!

In addition, next week you’ll practice answering questions a bit more. Questions during final presentations don’t tend to be as technical as questions during faculty reviews, but you should still be ready to answer about any details in your project.

Review this presentation about best practices to answer questions. Especially the “dos” and “don’ts”
Identify 4 - 5 bins that you expect questions to fall into (e.g. data management, statistical analyses, working with a client). Write them down.
Brainstorm who would be most appropriate to respond to questions in each bin.
Write down 2 - 3 questions in each bin that you might expect (and/or are nervous of getting) during your presentation
Prepare, if necessary, any extra slides you might need during your Q&A.

Make your best effort so that everyone in your team gets a chance to answer something. Specially the team members who are not presenting.





When? Due on Friday, May 23 at 5 PM.
How? Submit data’s DOI or explain why there is no data archival using this Google form.


Instructions

Friday, May 23 is the final due date to archive your capstone project data. This is an academic requirement.
Use this week and next to:

Discuss with your advisor or client what data (if any) should be archived as part of the project deliverables.
Read through the Archiving and Preserving Your Data information by the Library RDS team for detailed guidelines about what to archive and the submission process to Dryad.
If necessary, make an appointment with the RDS team (rds@library.ucsb.edu) to discuss archival of data associated with your capstone project.


When you submit data to Dryad (recommended repository) the data archival process won’t be complete yet, as your submission might need to be revised. However, you will get a DOI associated with your data. To fulfill the data archival requirement, you must submit your data’s DOI by Friday, May 23. If you are not archiving any data related to your capstone project, you must briefly explain why that is the case.












The class will be divided in two blocks from 1:00 to 2:30 and from 2:30 to 4:00. Each team will present and provide feedback to other teams as follows:

Your theam should work on their project outside of their “presentation block”. The following rooms are reserved for you so you have a dedicated workspace during our usual class time. You’re welcome to use other places to work.

title: Spring Week 3
text:
Next class 12:30-2:00 PM: Unit Testing Workshop



Workshop led by Sam Shanny-Csik on unit testing for dashboard development.

It will cover how to think about testing, regardless of the language. Conceptually, this will be relevant to all teams developing a dashboard.
All examples will be in R using testthat, shiny, and shinytest2.

Highly encouraged, but optional! Discuss with your team whether it is relevant for your project. It’s ok if some team members attend and others don’t.
We will all meet back in the classroom at 2:00 PM.

📥 Submit Technical Documentation Preliminary Draft
Due when? By 5 pm on Friday 3/17.
How to submit: Send an email to your advisor and 411 instructor with a link to your Google doc. You can continue working on the document in our shared TD Drafts folder.


Instructions


Make the most out of this revision: it’s great to request specific feedback from your advisor! Is there any section you’d particularly like their input on? Let them know in your message. You can also tag us in comments inside your document.

For each section:

include as much information as completed to date. Clearly indicated placeholders or bulleted lists are acceptable at this point.
note the extent to which the section is completed and any remaining obstacles to its completion.

As an appendix to the draft:

describe the work to be completed during the remainder of Spring Quarter
include a timeline for remaining tasks and deliverables.





🙌 3. ONGOING: Work on project implementation!

text:
To do by next week

1. 💬 Prepare practice presentation 2.0
Due when? Presentations will happen during next class on Tuesday 2/17.
How to submit: Add slides to Week 7 - Faculty Reviews DRAFT 2 shared folder.


Instructions

🎯 Prepare one or two slides with questions for your faculty reviewers and add them to your slide deck.

The instructor might send you some extra comments and feedback on your presentation today.
Assess and incorporate peer and instructor feedback and streamline your presentation.
Iterate with your faculty advisor.
Practice goes a long way!

Be sure to review the faculty review materials at the top of the page when preparing your presentation.




2. 🙋🏽‍♀️ Prepare for Q&A
Due when? We will practice answering questions after your presentation next class on Tuesday 2/17.


Instructions

Next week, we’ll practice answering questions a bit more.

Review this presentation about best practices to answer questions.
Identify 4 - 5 bins that you expect faculty review questions to fall into (e.g. model selection, data management, statistical analyses). Write them down.
Decide who would be most appropriate to respond to questions in each bin.
Write down 2 - 3 questions in each bin that you might expect (and/or are nervous of getting) during your faculty review.
Prepare any extra slides you might need during your Q&A.
Designate a note taker for the Q&A who can keep track of the questions, feedback, or comments you got for reviewing later.





3. 📝 Update DIP


Instructions

Once you receive feedback from your advisor:

discuss feedback with your team
start working on updating your draft
contact advisors or clients for clarification and share suggestions. Take the initiative suggsting solutions or approaches and reply to comments within the document as needed. Remember to always do your research and discuss with your team first!





4. 🤔 Have a mid-quarter check-in with team
Due when? Add this item to the agenda for your next team meeting.


Instructions

Context: your Team Management Plan + DIP draft submissions + flashtalks + practice presentation, and the feedback you received.
Discuss what went well and and what could be better:

✨How will your team improve?✨
Is your team delivering high-quality, timely work?
Do you need to re-read the capstone guidelines?
Should you seek more feedback or guidance from your client/advisor?
Should you be more proactive in proposing solutions or product details?
Does your team need to share work earlier so everyone can revise?
Do you need to establish new expectations or ground rules in your Team Management Plan?

title: Winter Week 2
text:
To do by next week

📥 1. Prepare summary paragraph for Bren website
Due when? Submit by Friday 1/16.
How to submit: Add to Capstone Summaries.  Your advisor must review your summary before submitting it. 
See week 1 class slides for details about how to prepare your summary.



🤝 2. Submit Team Management Plan draft
Due when? Submit by next class Tuesday 1/20.
How to submit: Add document to the Team Management Plan draft shared folder.


Instructions


Finish drafting the DIP Team Management Plan section using the Team Management Plan template.

Consider the following resources when writing the Team Management Plan:

MEDS Capstone Project Guidelines
Team’s project roles and responsibilities
Ten simple rules to cultivate belonging in collaborative data science research teams
Communication roles activity form last week
Dr. Allison Horst’s Code of Conduct

Additionally, check your “Expectations between students and advisor” section in Team Management Plan with your advisor. You can add this as a discussion item to the agenda for your next meeting with your advisor or email them asking for their feedback. If you are not sure how to bring this up try something like:

“Our Team Management Plan must include a section about expectations between students and faculty advisor. We have included some of these expectations and would like to discuss them with you. We also want to integrate expectations you have about how our team will conduct its work in this project.”

If you have already checked these expectations with your advisor, great! You’re done.




📝 3. Draft summary, objectives, and deliverables DIP sections
Due when? Submit by next class Tuesday 1/20.
How to submit: Add this document to the DIP drafts shared folder. This document will be used to draft all sections of the DIP except the Team Management Plan.


Instructions

Draft the following sections of the DIP (they should be short!):

Summary
Objectives
Deliverables

You can see more details about how to structure these sections on ths week’s class slides and the Capstone Guidelines.
Remember:

your team is responsible for reaching out to your client or advisor with any questions needed to clarify these sections
as needed, you can seek feedback from your advisor on your DIP sections before submitting a complete first draft on Friday 2/6.





📑 4. Prepare for data management meeting with Research Data Services
Due when? Submit before next class Tuesday 1/20.
How to submit: Add your answered questionnaire to the “RDS meeting questionnaire” shared folder.


Instructions

Next week, each team will meet independently with the Research Data Services (RDS) team from the UCSB Library to help you plan your Data Management Plan. Complete the following tasks in preparation for this meeting:

Read the Data Management Plan section of the MEDS Capstone Project Guidelines (1 page)
Answer this questionnaire prepared by RDS. This is an informal document, short answers are ok. If a question is not relevant you can skip it or write a short explanation why this does not apply.
Come prepared with questions about data management and archival for your project.





🧠 ONGOING: Work on data exploration and implementing your project!

text:
To do by next week

🤝 1. Hold first client meeting
Due when? By next class Monday 1/11.


Instructions

Goals for first client meeting (to be held before next class):
☑️ Clarify what the objectives are (if you have any questions - know what they are before the meeting)
☑️ Address any issue with accessing data (determine beforehand what these might be)
☑️ Find out who the audience for your product will be and if there will be people who can test it
Before meeting the client meeting, develop an agenda, including:

who will be the meeting facilitator/chair and who will take notes
agenda (with times)
list of clarifying questions you want to ask the client
any questions about the data - make sure you know how to access it
PRO TIP: use the agenda template provided during the Fall quarter.

After meeting: reflect with your team on how it went and what could be improved.




💬 2. Prepare flash talk
Due when? Presentations will happen during next class. Add your slides by then.
How to submit: Add slides to Week 2 - Flash talks shared folder.


Instructions

Prepare four slides covering:

Slide 1: A cover slide

Slide 2: An ultra summarized slide covering what your project will do
Slide 3: A brief overview of the datasets you’ll use
Slide 4: One graph showing exploratory visualization of your data

Pick a person or two who are ready to do a 3 minute presentation next class
Focus on:

Avoiding big blocks of text.
Smooth transitions between sections, clear, and precise speech.
Practice it a bit and time it!

Flash talks are the first steps in practicing for the faculty reviews.




📄 3. Read sample DIPs and Capstone resources and bring in questions
Due when? By next class.


Instructions

Read the following resources:
☑️ MEDS Capstone Project Guidelines.
☑️ Sample DIPs
☑️ Ten simple rules to cultivate belonging in collaborative data science research teams
Come prepared with questions about the guidelines and the sample DIPs. The “Ten simple rules…” paper will give you a good background for your team management plan (we’ll work on it next week).
*We have reworked the DIP this year to streamline it. So, the sample DIPs from previous years do not completely match the new DIP format. However they will still give you a good idea of the information that will go into it.

Due when? Submit by 5pm on Friday 1/16.
How to submit: add to Capstone Summaries.  Your advisor must review your summary before submitting it. 
See class slides for details about how to prepare your summary.

title: Overview
text:
Welcome to the course materials for EDS 411A - MEDS Capstone! This course is part of the UCSB Masters in Environmental Data Science at the Bren School of Environmental Science and Mananagement.
In this website you will find the materials covered during lectures for the Winter 2024 term.



The goal of EDS 411A is to facilitate student learning, work, and progress on their MEDS Capstone project through lessons, activities, supervised and unsupervised project work, and peer review and feedback. Additionally, EDS 411A provides opportunities for professional and career development (e.g. presentation skills, writing and editing, client relations, team science, panel discussions, and conflict resolution).



Click here to view syllabus.



Carmen Galaz García
Pronouns: she/her/hers
E-mail: c_galazgarcia@ucsb.edu
Office hour: by appointmnet, Zoom or NCEAS classroom
Best way to contact me: e-mail, Slack



The following is our ideal calendar, the course content may be subject to change as the course progresses.

Students held a post-mortem meeting to discuss how their team worked during winter quarter. The goal was to identify lessons learned and ideas to improve going forward.
We went over what to expect in 411B: fewer lectures and brainstorms, more peer testing, more dedicated work time, continuing presentation and public speaking practice.
Students established expectations for work during spring break and finals week and about when they will resume their project.
I asked everyone to schedule their team and advisor weekly meetings and they should revise their DIP timeline and discuss it with their advisors at the latest on week 1 or Spring quarter.

Tasks all groups are expected to complete before the end of this week (Friday 3/13)

Send final DIP to 411 instructor, advisor, and client.
Submit Peer/Self evaluation form and send a copy to their advisor.
Finish set up for Spring quarter.
Fill out UCSB 411A course survey.

📝 1. Draft of DIP Solution Design section, including Approach, Methods, and Research Data.
Due when? Submit by next class Monday 1/26.
How to submit: Add section to your DIP draft in the DIP drafts shared folder.


Instructions

Add all sections of the Solution Design to your DIP draft in the DIP drafts shared folder.
Include your workflow diagram draft (a screenshot is ok). This should become a finalized figure with an overview of your project’s workflow for the final draft due on week 5.
There might be details, questions, or even gaps about how you’ll create your deliverables. Start filling those in. First step is always to research, brainstorm, and clarify with your team. Then, as needed, reach out to your advisor or client with questions or options.




🗓️ 2. Draft DIP Timeline & Milestones
Due when? Submit by next class Monday 1/26.
How to submit: Add section to your DIP draft in the DIP drafts shared folder.


Instructions

Review the class slides about the DIP Timeline and milestones.
Include a Gantt Chart to visualize represent your project’s timeline. Some Gantt chart resources from Dr. Casey O’Hara. You can also make a nice looking one in Google sheets.
Timeline should include client’s and academic deliverables, and appropriate intermediate deliverables leading to the project’s completion
Add these sections to your DIP draft in the DIP drafts shared folder.




💬 3. Prepare flash talk
Due when? Presentations will happen during next class on Monday 1/26.
How to submit: Add slides to Week 4 - Flash talks shared folder.


Instructions

Prepare 4 slides covering:

Slide 1: A cover slide (see template)
Slide 2: Your ABT elevator pitch telling us what your project is about (30 seconds max)

Your ABT slide should look like the in-class examples: include “and”, “but”, “therefore”
Audience for ABT pitch: general audience (think anyone at Bren)

Slide 3: Brief overview of project’s products and deliverables
Slide 4: Approach and methods draft diagram

Pick a person or two who are ready to do a 3 minute presentation next class.
Add slides to Week 4 - Flash talks before class.
Focus on:

Avoiding big blocks of text.
Make sure font is not too small.
Practice it a bit and time it.
Listen for every qualifier you use. “Kind of” “sort of” “I guess.” Try to avoid these!
When describing your workflow diagram, practice curious statements. Instead of “We have no idea how we’re going to do this part,” what can you say?
Project your voice!
Ask for questions at the end of the presentation





🧼 4. Add summary to GitHub organization, delete demo repos, and make all members public
Due when? By next class Monday 1/26.
How to submit: Verify the URL to your GitHub organization on this spreadsheet is correct and update if needed.


Instructions

Update your GitHub organization’s landing page to include the project summary you submitted on week 2. Delete all the repositories, issues, and projects from this week’s demo sessions. Make all members in your organization public members, so people can see your GitHub profiles when they visit your org.
As you create repositories and work in your organization, follow the Checklist for MEDS Capstone Projects Organizations and Repositories.

2. 🎤 Prepare final presentation mock presentation
Due when? Due by next class Monday 5/11.
How to submit: Add slides to shared Google drive.


Instructions


Decide who will be presenting in the final public presentation. Remember every person in the team should be ready to present if needed.

Strongly suggest following Alex’s presentation, point 6 to prepare your slides.
It’s ok if you add stand-in figures for prototypes and results for now.
Focus on the story line: this is a public presentation for a general audience.


Discuss your draft slides with your faculty advisor.
Add your draft to our shared drive and be ready to do present next week!





3. 📥 Submit final abstract and acknowledgements
Due when? Due on Friday 5/15.
How to submit: Upload to this shared Google drive as Word (.doc) or Google doc format, not PDF.


Instructions

Abstracts and acknowledgments will be posted to the Bren website on your individual project pages and linked to the digital event program.

Upload your abstract and acknowledgments in a single file to this shared Google drive.
Include your group name in the file name.
Word (.doc) or Google doc format please, not PDF (must be copy & paste-able).

Abstract instructions:

The abstract is a one-paragraph brief summary of the Capstone Project, meant for a non-technical audience. The length of the abstract should be roughly 200 words. It can be the same as the one in your Technical Documentation.
If your project title has changed, please include the new title in the abstract.
Your advisor must approve the final abstract and any title updates before submission.

Acknowledgments instructions:

The acknowledgements should include advisors, professionals, organizations, funding sources, etc. that assisted the Capstone Project.
Each acknowledgment should include the person’s name, title (if known), and affiliation or organization.
You should obtain authorization from each person or party being acknowledged that their names can be included in the abstract.
Be consistent when listing your acknowledgments. You can list several people within an organization. Alphabetical order is preferred if listing several people in the same grouping.

Example 1: Joan Dudney, Assistant Professor, Bren School
Example 2: Bren School: Max Czapanskiy, Assistant Teaching Professor; Roland Geyer, Professor; Ruth Oliver, Assistant Professor
Example 3: The Nature Conservancy: Kelly Easterday, Heather Gately, Moses Katkowski, Diego Ortiz, Dr. Mark Reynolds, and Dr. Rae Wynn-Grant


Tip: You can view Bren faculty titles here. Also, if you have an email from someone, you may find their title in their email signature.




4. 📊 Initiate data archival
Friday 5/22 is the final due date to archive your capstone project data. This is an academic requirement.
Use this week and next to:

Discuss with your advisor or client what data (if any) should be archived as part of the project deliverables.
If necessary, make an appointment with the RDS team (rds@library.ucsb.edu) to discuss archival of data associated with your capstone project.

Before contacting the Library RDS team or archiving your data, please read through the Archiving and Preserving Your Data information for detailed guidelines about what to archive and the submission process to Dryad.

title: 411B Advisor Emails
text:
Week 1
Hi MEDS Capstone Advisors,
I hope you had a re-energizing Spring break and your quarter is off to a great start!
I’m looking forward to keeping working together as students advance on their capstone projects. Like last quarter, I’ll send you a weekly email with updates about what we’re covering in the MEDS Capstone Course 411B and reminders of upcoming deadlines. This one is a bit late as you’ll normally receive it Mondays.
Please don’t hesitate to reach out with any questions, comments, or suggestions.
Best wishes,
Carmen

Carmen Galaz García | Assistant Teaching Professor
Bren School of Environmental Science and Management
University of California, Santa Barbara
(she / her / hers)


EDS 411 Course website
Capstone guidelines
Upcoming Major Deadlines 




Advisor Check-ins

This quarter’s written academic deliverable is the Technical Documentation (TD). Students will be working towards a complete draft due on week 7 and will have the flexibility to adapt this document to best serve their project’s purpose. Please read section 4.B (pages 26-33) in the Capstone guidelines and check this week’s slides for details.
Students will prepare a detailed list of the sections they’ll include in their TD and might contact you for feedback about this.
Students will review the timeline they submitted as part of the DIP to assess whether they are on schedule to complete their project and academic deliverables. They should discuss any significant changes in their timeline with you.



In 411B this week (slides @ website)

We covered an overview of EDS 411B, this quarter’s timeline, and the spring quarter deliverables.
We covered details about the Technical Documentation and its sections. Students read the corresponding information in the Capstone Guidelines.
Students worked independently with their capstone team and I checked in with each team.


Tasks all groups are expected to complete before next 411B class:

Write down a detailed list of the sections (table of contents) for their Technical Documentation (TD), specifying who is the target audience for each section or groups of sections.
Write down a timeline to completion for the Technical Documentation.
Write down information for their executive summary about the background, motivation, problem statement, objectives, and deliverables.
Revise the project timeline they submitted in the DIP and discuss major changes with their advisor.

Advisor Check-ins

Students will submit a preliminary draft of their Technical Documentation this coming Friday 3/17. Expect an email with a link to a Google doc. Feedback from advisors is due on Friday 3/24.
What to expect in this TD draft? From the Capstone Guidelines:


Each section of the preliminary draft must include as much information as completed to date. Clearly indicated placeholders or bulleted lists are acceptable at this point. An appendix to the preliminary draft should describe the work to be completed during the remainder of Spring Quarter, any remaining obstacles to its completion, and a timeline for remaining tasks and deliverables.


As you review your team’s TD, please take the following into account:

Pages 24-33 of the Capstone Guidelines have extensive details about what is expected on each section of the Technical Documentation.
Please encourage students to be specific and detailed.
The TD format is flexible, so if you feel aspects of the project are not being communicated clearly, feel free to suggest restructuring.

Encourage your team to report values and include formulas, figures, and tables to communicate the project’s methods and results.
